nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater rivers and tributaries; slow to still water with humic-stained, highly acidic, soft water; surface and mid-water zones near overhanging or floating vegetation and submerged wood

decorPreferences: floating or overhanging vegetation for surface cover
driftwood and roots to break line of sight
open swimming lanes at the surface
tight-fitting, secured lid — obligatory due to very high escape risk
avoid sharp decorations that can injure a panicking fish

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in the wild; feeds on smaller fishes and large insects. Newly acquired specimens nearly always require live feeder fish initially. With patience, many individuals can be transitioned to dead/frozen whole fish, large earthworms, whole prawns, mussels, strips of fish flesh, and crickets. Frozen or fresh alternatives should be offered on a feeding stick to simulate prey movement. Avoid feeder goldfish (disease vector and poor nutrition) and mammalian or avian meats. Feed every 2–3 days; remove uneaten food promptly.

goodWith: similarly-sized or larger Geophagus and Satanoperca cichlids
other large peaceful South American fish too big to swallow
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed whole
fast, boisterous fish that will startle and injure this skittish species
small tetras, nano fish, dwarf cichlids, or small shrimp

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Boulengerella lateristriga; check current FishBase page for updated image URL and licensing.

summary: A sleek, surface-dwelling piscivore from Amazonian blackwater rivers reaching 30 cm, related to the Hujeta Gar. Demands very large, covered tanks, soft acidic water, and live or fresh whole-fish diet. Specialist-only species.
careNotes: The Striped Pike Characin is a member of the family Ctenoluciidae — the pike-characins — closely related to the Hujeta Gar (Ctenolucius hujeta, fw-fish-377). It inhabits the Rio Negro basin of Brazil and Colombia and the upper Río Orinoco of Venezuela, living in humic-stained blackwater with very low mineral content and pH commonly below 5. In the aquarium, conditions should reflect this: pH 4.0–7.0, very soft water (gH 0–10, kH 0–4), temperature 22–28°C, and excellent dissolved-oxygen levels maintained by moderate surface movement and strong filtration (4–5× turnover). Peat filtration or blackwater extract is strongly advisable to reproduce the low-pH, tannin-rich environment and reduce stress-related disease. A minimum tank of 500 L with a footprint of at least 180 cm is required for an adult specimen; SeriouslyFish recommends 240 × 90 cm base dimensions for a group. This species is an obligate surface predator that patrols the upper water column; the aquarium must be covered with a tightly fitted, weighted lid — Boulengerella lateristriga is a powerful and unpredictable jumper. Avoid sharp decor: startled fish ricochet violently and can suffer lacerations. Subdued or diffused lighting and floating plants reduce stress and skittish behaviour. Best kept in groups of four or more juveniles grown on together, as conspecific aggression is low when fish are similarly matched in size. Newly acquired specimens almost exclusively accept live feeder fish; conversion to dead/thawed whole fish or large invertebrates (prawns, mussels, earthworms) is possible but requires patience and a feeding stick to simulate movement. Feeder goldfish should be avoided due to disease risk and thiaminase content; instead use gut-loaded livebearers or small trout/tilapia strips. Weekly water changes of 30–50% are recommended to maintain the low-TDS blackwater conditions this species requires. Not suitable for community tanks with smaller fish. Tankmates must be large enough not to be eaten and calm enough not to trigger panicked escape attempts.
breedingNotes: No breeding has been recorded in captivity. Wild reproductive biology is poorly documented. Given the large tank requirements and specialist diet demands, captive breeding is considered essentially impractical outside of institutional facilities.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Boulengerella lateristriga
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (max recorded 258 mm SL), water parameters (22–28°C, pH 4.0–7.0, hardness 18–179 ppm), minimum tank dimensions (240 × 90 cm base, ~1944 L), diet (obligate carnivore; live fish to start, can transition to frozen/fresh meaty foods), native range (Rio Negro, Brazil/Colombia; upper Río Orinoco, Venezuela), habitat (blackwater with pH 3.0–4.0 naturally), tankmates (Geophagus, Acestrorhynchus, doradid/loricariid catfishes), grouping (4+), breeding (unrecorded), and escapeRisk/cover notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Boulengerella lateristriga
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (25.8 cm SL, 74.7 g), temperature (23–27°C), pH (5.2–6.8), native range (Rio Negro, Amazonas, Brazil; upper Río Orinoco, Venezuela), family (Ctenoluciidae), order (Characiformes), diet (carnivore, trophic level 4.2), IUCN status (Least Concern). Note: FishBase max SL of 25.8 cm is a recorded specimen minimum; sources including SeriouslyFish and trade literature consistently report 30+ cm TL in aquaria, so 30 cm used as practical max.

additional.name: Aquatic Community — Striped Pike Characin
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size up to 40 cm (likely TL including caudal; used 30 cm as conservative aquarium max), temperature 23–28°C, pH 5.0–6.8, diet (prefers live food but accepts beefheart, shrimp, prawns), temperament (aggressive predator, skittish), breeding (not recorded in home aquaria), native range (Rio Negro, Rio Urubu), tankmate guidance (calm species too large to be eaten).

additional.name: The Aquarium Wiki — Boulengerella lateristriga
additional.notes: Supplementary husbandry detail: minimum 284 L tank (used as minimum for single juvenile; 500 L adopted for adult/group per SeriouslyFish), pH 5.2–6.8, temperature 23–27°C, hardness 8–10 dH, skittish behaviour notes, tight-fitting cover requirement confirmed, weekly 30–50% water changes recommended.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding on record. Wild spawning biology undescribed. Institutional-scale facilities with very large tanks would be the only realistic setting for breeding attempts.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 30+ cm; surface piscivore needing 500L+ tank; high escape risk; live-feeder-dependent. Specialist large-fish setup.
